worth_it_explanation: ads.txt and app-ads.txt are the public record that tells buyers which sellers, e.g., SpringServe, are authorized to sell a publisher's video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory. A missing line, a wrong account identifier, or a stale designation can cause demand-side platforms to drop bids on inventory they cannot verify, so the impressions go unsold or clear at a lower price. The check is a review of text files the publisher already controls, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific line to add, correct, or remove.
intro: ads.txt and app-ads.txt are plain-text files a publisher hosts to declare which companies may sell its inventory. For a publisher serving video and CTV ads through SpringServe, these files name SpringServe as an authorized seller with the correct account identifier and the right relationship type. This subcategory is about whether those authorization records are accurate and current, rather than about how SpringServe routes demand once a bid is authorized. These checks confirm that the SpringServe authorization line is present, correct, and unaccompanied by stale or unauthorized entries that weaken the file's value to buyers.

audit_checks.title: The SpringServe authorization line is missing or carries the wrong account ID or relationship type
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A buyer's demand-side platform reads ads.txt and app-ads.txt to confirm that the seller offering an impression is authorized for that domain or app. If the SpringServe line is absent, names the wrong account identifier, or carries the wrong DIRECT or RESELLER designation, the bid request fails that verification and many buyers drop it, so authorized inventory goes unsold or clears below its value across every site and app the file should cover.

educate.base:
ads.txt and app-ads.txt are public text files that let a publisher declare, in a form any buyer can read, which companies are authorized to sell its inventory. A website hosts ads.txt at the root of its domain. An app declares its authorized sellers through app-ads.txt, hosted on the developer's website that the app store listing points to, which is the path the IAB Tech Lab specification defines for apps distributed through mobile and Connected TV (CTV) app stores. For a publisher that serves video and CTV ads through SpringServe, both files need a line that names SpringServe as an authorized seller.

Each authorization line carries three parts that a buyer's demand-side platform checks. The first is the seller's canonical domain, which identifies the advertising system, e.g., the exchange or ad server. The second is the publisher's account identifier within that seller, the value that ties the inventory back to this specific publisher's account. The third is the relationship type, either DIRECT when the publisher's own account sells the inventory or RESELLER when an authorized third party resells it on the publisher's behalf. A buyer that cannot match all three against the bid request treats the impression as unverified.

The failure modes here are concrete. A line can be absent entirely, so the publisher's SpringServe inventory is sold by a seller the file never authorized. The account identifier can be wrong, copied from another property or mistyped, so it points at no account or the wrong one. The designation can be wrong, marking reseller inventory as DIRECT or the reverse, which misrepresents who actually controls the sale. Any of these breaks verification for the buyers that enforce ads.txt, and many do.

Confirming the line takes the exact values from the seller, not a guess. SpringServe supplies the canonical domain, the publisher's account identifier, and the correct relationship type for that account, and those values belong in both the ads.txt for each website and the app-ads.txt for each app. A few details are worth confirming against the seller's record:
  • The seller domain matches the canonical domain SpringServe publishes, with no typo or trailing path
  • The account identifier matches the one tied to this publisher's SpringServe account exactly
  • The relationship is DIRECT or RESELLER according to how the account actually sells, not a default copied from another line
You may want to confirm the SpringServe line against the values SpringServe provides for the account, then check that the same line is present in every site's ads.txt and every app's app-ads.txt.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster serving CTV and online video, the same content often reaches viewers through a website, a mobile app, and apps on CTV platforms. Each surface has its own authorization file, so the website needs ads.txt and each app needs an app-ads.txt reachable from its store listing. You may want to confirm the SpringServe line appears in all of them rather than only on the website. CTV app inventory is sold through app-ads.txt, and a missing app file leaves that inventory unverified.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running video on product, category, and editorial pages, the ads.txt at the domain root covers the whole site including the commercial video inventory. You may want to confirm the SpringServe line is present in that single domain-root file and that no separate subdomain hosting the video player needs its own ads.txt. A buyer checks the file at the root of the domain in the bid request.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for the exact seller domain, account identifier, and relationship type to declare is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the ads.txt for each website and the app-ads.txt for each app, then confirm the SpringServe line is present with the seller domain, account identifier, and relationship type that match the values from SpringServe.

browser.label: Web browser
steps: Open the ads.txt file for each website by visiting the domain root path '/ads.txt', e.g., 'https://example.com/ads.txt'.
For each app, find the developer website listed on the app store page, then open '/app-ads.txt' at the root of that website.
Locate the line or lines naming SpringServe and record the seller domain, the account identifier, and the relationship type, either DIRECT or RESELLER.

Compare each recorded value against the seller domain, account identifier, and relationship type SpringServe provides for the account, and note any line that is missing, mistyped, or carries the wrong designation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each website and app, whether a SpringServe authorization line is present and whether its seller domain, account identifier, and relationship type match the values SpringServe provides for the account. A file that carries the correct line on every surface passes. A file missing the line, or carrying a wrong account identifier or a wrong DIRECT or RESELLER designation, is a fail recorded with the surface and the specific value that does not match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the SpringServe line for each website and app, with its seller domain, account identifier, and relationship type, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is missing an accurate SpringServe authorization line in its ads.txt or app-ads.txt, or carries one with the wrong account identifier or relationship type. Buyers read these files to confirm that the seller offering an impression is authorized for the domain or app, so a missing or incorrect line causes many demand-side platforms to drop the bid, and the inventory goes unsold or clears below its value. {{voice_recommend}} taking the exact seller domain, account identifier, and DIRECT or RESELLER designation from SpringServe and confirming the matching line is present in the ads.txt for every website and the app-ads.txt for every app. After the correction, recheck each file to confirm the line reads exactly as the seller record specifies.

audit_checks.title: Stale, duplicate, or unauthorized lines remain in the file
audit_checks.impact_rationale: ads.txt and app-ads.txt grow as sellers are added over time, and lines for sellers no longer in use, duplicate entries, or sellers that were never authorized accumulate unless they are pruned. Stale and unauthorized lines widen the set of sellers a buyer treats as authorized, which weakens the file's value as a fraud control and can let inventory be sold through paths the publisher no longer controls.

educate.base:
An ads.txt or app-ads.txt file does the most good when every line in it is a seller the publisher actually authorizes today. The file is additive by habit. A new exchange or ad server gets a line when it is brought on, but lines for sellers that have since been dropped are rarely removed, duplicate lines pile up when the same seller is pasted twice, and lines copied from a template or a partner's example can name sellers that were never authorized for this inventory at all.

Each kind of leftover line carries its own cost. A stale line for a seller no longer in use tells buyers that seller may still sell the inventory, which keeps a path open that the publisher meant to close. A duplicate line adds noise and, if the two copies disagree on the account identifier or the DIRECT or RESELLER designation, sends buyers conflicting signals about the same seller. An unauthorized line, one for a seller the publisher never engaged, directly undermines the purpose of the file since it vouches for a seller the publisher cannot stand behind.

For a publisher serving video and Connected TV (CTV) ads through SpringServe, the SpringServe line itself should be present and correct, and the lines around it should be just as deliberate. The audit question is whether every other line in the file still reflects an active, authorized seller relationship, or whether the file has accumulated entries that no longer belong.

Pruning the file is a matter of reconciling each line against the publisher's current seller relationships. A few entries are worth flagging for review:
  • Lines for sellers the publisher no longer works with, which can be removed once confirmed inactive
  • Duplicate lines for the same seller, especially any that disagree on the account identifier or relationship type
  • Lines for sellers the publisher cannot confirm authorizing, which warrant a check before they are removed
You may want to reconcile the file against the publisher's current list of authorized sellers, then remove the stale and duplicate lines and investigate any entry no one can account for.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, authorized-seller relationships shift as the publisher changes exchanges and ad servers across its website and CTV apps, so each surface's file can carry leftovers from sellers no longer in use. You may want to reconcile every ads.txt and app-ads.txt against the current seller list rather than assuming the apps inherit the website's cleanup. Each file is maintained on its own.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, the domain-root ads.txt may carry lines added during past tests with sellers that were never kept, alongside the active SpringServe and other authorized lines. You may want to reconcile the file against the sellers actually in use today and remove the entries left over from sellers that were trialed and dropped.

investigate.intro: Review every line in the ads.txt for each website and the app-ads.txt for each app, then reconcile each against the publisher's current list of authorized sellers to find stale, duplicate, or unrecognized entries.

browser.label: Web browser
steps: Open the ads.txt at each website's domain root and the app-ads.txt for each app, and copy the full contents of each file.
List every seller line with its seller domain, account identifier, and relationship type, then group the lines by seller to surface any duplicates.
Compare the list of sellers against the publisher's current record of authorized seller relationships, and mark any seller no longer in use as stale.
Flag any line for a seller the publisher cannot confirm authorizing as unrecognized, and record any duplicate lines that disagree on the account identifier or relationship type.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every line in each file and reconciles it against the publisher's current list of authorized sellers. A file where every line maps to an active, authorized seller with no duplicates passes. A file containing lines for sellers no longer in use, duplicate lines for the same seller, or lines for sellers the publisher cannot confirm authorizing is a fail recorded with each flagged line and the reason it does not belong.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each line in the file and whether it maps to a current authorized seller above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt or app-ads.txt for {{site_name}} carries stale, duplicate, or unauthorized seller lines alongside the active ones. Leftover lines widen the set of sellers a buyer treats as authorized, which weakens the file as a fraud control and can keep selling paths open that {{site_name}} no longer controls. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling every line against the current list of authorized sellers, removing the entries for sellers no longer in use, collapsing duplicate lines for the same seller, and confirming any unrecognized line before deciding whether to keep or remove it. After the cleanup, recheck each file so every remaining line maps to a seller {{site_name}} authorizes today.
